findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan to note that `plan_review_decide_input.schema.json` currently has `additionalProperties: true` (open, not closed) and that atomically setting `additionalProperties: false` when adding `review_phase` is required work — not a pre-existing condition.
evidence: plan_review_decide_input.schema.json line 14: `"additionalProperties": true` — schema is OPEN
plan_review_precheck_output.schema.json line 17: `"additionalProperties": false` — that schema IS closed
Plan text: 'both closed schemas plan_review_precheck_output.schema.json and plan_review_decide_input.schema.json'
AC line: 'Closed-schema golden tests prove review_phase is required in both declared schemas and schema/YAML/code rollback is version-coherent'
findings.finding: `plan_review_decide_input.schema.json` has `"additionalProperties": true` (it is an OPEN schema), but the plan's Implementation section and AC call it a 'closed schema' and plan to 'update their properties and required arrays atomically' — closing it and adding `review_phase` must be done in one step, but the plan treats it as already-closed which is incorrect.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Add required `review_phase` with string enum `planning|execution` to both closed schemas `plan_review_precheck_output.schema.json` and `plan_review_decide_input.schema.json`' / AC: 'Closed-schema golden tests prove `review_phase` is required in both declared schemas'
scenarios: The AC says 'closed-schema golden tests' for both schemas, but the decide_input schema currently has additionalProperties: true — a golden test comparing the closed contract will pass today for fields other than review_phase without rejecting unknown keys, providing false confidence that the schema is strict.
If the atomic change forgets to flip additionalProperties: false on plan_review_decide_input.schema.json, the schema is functionally open and the AC's 'closed-schema' framing is never validated.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Implementation section, change 'both closed schemas' to 'both output schemas (plan_review_precheck_output.schema.json, which is already closed, and plan_review_decide_input.schema.json, which must be closed atomically)'. Add an explicit AC: 'plan_review_decide_input.schema.json has additionalProperties: false after this change is applied'.

evidence: 36 acceptance-criteria items (> 25)
findings.finding: Oversize signals suggest this unit may be too large for one session.
findings.suggested_fix: Split into smaller child tickets, each a coherent single outcome.

evidence: 8 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a 'pause compact during rollback window' operator instruction to the rollback section (mirroring the I9a pattern in docs/concurrency.md), specify the exact post-rollback SNAPSHOT schema state for each ticket state (snapshot-replayed / never-compacted / old-code-compacted), and either add a recovery path (e.g., a fsck SNAPSHOT_STALE_PHASE detector + rebuild path) or explicitly assert that bootstrap-from-status recovery is the accepted and complete back-out.
evidence: The plan says 'old reducers preserve the unknown snapshot field during re-compaction' — this is only safe when old compact replays an existing SNAPSHOT via process_snapshot's generic for-loop (confirmed true in code). But when old code hits a cache miss and fully re-reduces from raw events, the resulting compiled_state lacks plan_review_phase, and old compact writes a SNAPSHOT without it.

The existing I9a pattern for creation_channel in docs/concurrency.md §I9a explicitly instructs: 'STOP every rebar compact invocation for the entire downgrade window … With compact paused, no ticket can enter state 3, so the downgrade is fully lossless.' No equivalent instruction appears in this plan's rollback section.

The plan says 'operators accept that documented loss of historical phase precision during the rollback window' without specifying the trigger condition (cache miss forcing full re-reduction) or a recovery path (e.g., fsck --repair-snapshots) analogous to the creation_channel pattern.

AC: 'The rollback test proves old-code raw-event re-reduction drops the unknown field and forward upgrade reboots it from compiled status, with the documented precision-loss window.' — the test acknowledges but does not prevent the loss; the plan provides no operator back-out.
findings.finding: The rollback section does not specify the required 'pause compact' operator instruction during the rollback window, nor the exact post-rollback SNAPSHOT schema state for tickets whose plan_review_phase-bearing SNAPSHOTs might be re-compacted by old code after rollback — leaving a data-loss path with no documented back-out.
findings.location: Implementation — final paragraph ('Rollback disables claim/close enforcement first; old reducers preserve the unknown snapshot field during re-compaction. If old code fully re-reduces raw events after its own cache bump, the phase field is absent...')
scenarios: Operator rolls back after shipping: old binary runs compact on a ticket whose SNAPSHOT already has plan_review_phase; old code's own cache-version bump (from any unrelated prior change) triggers a full raw-event re-reduction; resulting new SNAPSHOT lacks plan_review_phase; forward upgrade bootstraps from compiled status (open→planning, in_progress→execution) but loses the actual recorded phase for a ticket that was, e.g., execution-reviewed while compiled as open.

Scheduled compact runs during the rollback window (no pause instruction exists); multiple tickets lose phase precision permanently from their SNAPSHOTs; fsck has no SNAPSHOT_STALE_PHASE analog to surface the affected tickets.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a subsection to the Rollback paragraph modeled on docs/concurrency.md §I9a: (1) 'During the rollback window, pause all rebar compact invocations. A phase-bearing SNAPSHOT replayed by old code is safe (process_snapshot passes unknown fields through verbatim). A ticket never compacted after upgrade is safe (plan_review_phase is projected from raw events on re-reduction by old code is absent, but the CREATE is retained). A ticket whose cache misses and is re-reduced from raw events by old code during the rollback window loses plan_review_phase from its SNAPSHOT durably.' (2) 'After re-upgrading, rebar fsck SNAPSHOT_STALE_PHASE (new fsck check) surfaces affected tickets; fsck --repair-snapshots re-reduces them with new code to restore the field.' Alternatively, explicitly accept the loss and document that bootstrap-from-compiled-status is the complete recovery.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either make the execution floor configurable (e.g. a named protocol version or a floor field in the generation transaction), or add a dedicated ADR/section in `docs/plan-review-gate.md` describing the versioning strategy and migration runbook for future floor changes, so the hardcoding is an intentional, documented tradeoff rather than a silent constraint.
evidence: 'The fixed 0.80 protocol policy is not configurable.'
'Raising it later invalidates lower-floor execution attestations; lowering it accepts higher-floor attestations without migration.'
ABSENCE: no floor-versioning field in PlanReviewGeneration, no migration AC, no ADR entry for the version-consequence tradeoff
findings.finding: The 0.80 priority floor is explicitly hardcoded as a non-configurable protocol constant, yet the plan itself documents two concrete change scenarios (raising invalidates historical attestations; lowering accepts them without migration). No migration path or versioning mechanism is provided for either scenario, meaning any future policy adjustment requires manual attestation archaeology or a forced re-review wave.
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraph 4: 'The fixed 0.80 protocol policy is not configurable. Raising it later invalidates lower-floor execution attestations; lowering it accepts higher-floor attestations without migration.'
scenarios: Policy tightens to 0.85: all existing 0.80–0.84 execution attestations silently become incompatible with no tooling to identify or re-attest them.
Policy loosens to 0.70: old 0.80-floor attestations are accepted without any indication they were produced under stricter criteria, eroding audit trail integrity.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `floor_protocol_version` (e.g. integer, starting at 1) to both `PlanReviewGeneration` and the manifest line (`priority-floor-version: 1`). Compatibility checks compare version, not raw float, so raising the floor increments the version and only breaks attestations signed under an earlier version — making the impact visible and toolable. Document this in `docs/plan-review-gate.md` under a 'Floor versioning and migration' subsection.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate all direct/legacy callers of `plan_review_decide_input` schema validation and either include them in the atomic-ship list or document an explicit expand-contract sequence (e.g., make `review_phase` optional-with-default first, then required in a follow-on step).
evidence: 'Add required `review_phase` with string enum `planning|execution` to both closed schemas `plan_review_precheck_output.schema.json` and `plan_review_decide_input.schema.json`, updating their `properties` and `required` arrays atomically.'
'direct unit/legacy callers may use the default false only when they intentionally model planning'
Plan's rollback coverage says 'schema/YAML/code rollback is version-coherent' but does not enumerate nor migrate non-workflow callers that construct or validate the input schema directly.
findings.finding: Adding `review_phase` as a required field to both closed schemas (`plan_review_precheck_output.schema.json`, `plan_review_decide_input.schema.json`) is a breaking change for any caller that directly constructs or validates these schemas outside the orchestrated workflow path. The plan's 'atomic ship' argument covers the main workflow, but acknowledges 'direct unit/legacy callers' exist; these callers need an explicit enumeration or expand-contract sequence, not just the golden test and the default-false escape hatch on the Python function.
findings.location: Implementation — 'Thread phase through every Pass-3 entry'; AC: 'Closed-schema golden tests prove review_phase is required in both declared schemas and schema/YAML/code rollback is version-coherent.'
scenarios: A unit test or CLI tool that directly invokes plan_review_decide with a hand-crafted JSON payload (built before the schema change) will fail schema validation after the upgrade, with no documented migration path.
During a partial rollback where the schema is reverted but some callers have already been updated to emit review_phase, the inverse breakage occurs — also undocumented.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief rationale in the plan (or the docs section) explaining why the `PlanReviewGeneration` equality check under lock is necessary BEYOND the existing `RebaseGuard`/`LockTimeout` atomic-append mechanism, naming the specific failure mode (e.g. own-material drift or relation-snapshot drift between LLM call and lock acquisition) that the generation check catches.
evidence: Plan: 'Under the global write lock, require the same clean HEAD and exact generation, then append and commit the signature atomically.'
Existing code: `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py` already provides atomic append under lock with `RebaseGuard` on concurrent HEAD mutation.
Existing code: `src/rebar/_store/lock.py:LockTimeout` already handles lock contention.
Plan introduces a NEW retry-on-unstable-HEAD loop (up to 3 retries) that is not present anywhere in the codebase today.
ABSENCE: No prior `PlanReviewGeneration`, `PlanRelationSnapshot`, or `ticket_store_revision` exists in the codebase.
findings.finding: The `PlanReviewGeneration` transaction introduces a "collect generation → LLM → compare generation under lock" optimistic-concurrency protocol, but the plan doesn't justify why this is necessary beyond what the existing `event_append.py` + `LockTimeout` / `RebaseGuard` atomic-append-under-lock mechanism already provides. The plan doesn't demonstrate a failure mode the existing lock can't catch.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Own one pre-sign generation transaction' and 'Before signing, retain one immutable initial_generation' paragraphs
scenarios: If the existing RebaseGuard already serialises concurrent writers, the generation-equality check is redundant — operators and maintainers will be confused about why both mechanisms exist.

If the generation check is genuinely needed (e.g. to guard own-material staleness BETWEEN LLM call and lock acquisition), that specific gap should be stated so reviewers can confirm the retry count and behavior are calibrated correctly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a feature flag (e.g. `plan_review_execution_floor_enabled`) that gates the 0.80 floor enforcement, so it can be enabled per-tenant/canary before full rollout and toggled off without a code deploy.
evidence: No flag or canary is mentioned anywhere in the plan.
The Rollback section describes what old code does, but never a flag to gate the new blocking behavior.
The enforcement change (blocking findings below 0.80 in execution phase) affects all in-flight reviews the moment the code ships.
findings.finding: The plan introduces new execution-phase blocking enforcement (0.80 floor) as a single 100%-traffic flip with no feature flag, canary, or staged rollout path.
findings.location: Implementation — general; Rollback paragraph; AC: 'A production workflow integration test starts from compiled execution phase...'
scenarios: An active plan-review workflow in execution phase that was previously passing would suddenly be blocked at 0.80 with no graduated exposure window.
A sudden rollout bug in phase detection could block all execution-phase reviews site-wide with no fast toggle to disable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Cite a specific concurrency incident, scale estimate, or profiling result that motivates the 3-retry OCC transaction, or replace it with the existing sequential sign path plus a simple idempotent staleness check.
evidence: Codebase grep for 'LockTimeout', 'ticket_store_revision', 'tracker_head_sha', 'plan_review_generation_retry' returns zero results — none of these concurrency primitives exist today.
Existing `sign_plan_review` (attest.py:74-135) is a sequential read → manifest-build → sign_manifest call with no lock, no retry, no HEAD re-read — and no reported collision issue.
The plan contains no scale estimate, no profiling data, and no cited incident that motivated the retry loop.
G-9 scale-calibration: absent a scale citation, the small-scale default applies; a 3-retry OCC transaction is not warranted.
findings.finding: The pre-sign optimistic-concurrency transaction (read HEAD → collect generation → read HEAD again; 3-retry outer loop; under-lock re-verify; `LockTimeout` handling) introduces a bespoke concurrency-control scheme with no scale citation, no profiling result, and no existing evidence of collision problems in the current `sign_plan_review` path — classic premature-optimization.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Before signing, retain one immutable initial_generation ... Retry outside the lock at most three times ...' paragraph; AC lines 'HEAD instability retries at most three times', 'Under-lock mismatch retries; LockTimeout returns retryable unsigned'
scenarios: Implementers must build, test, and maintain a retry loop and structured-warn logging path for a code path with no documented concurrency problem.
The retry machinery interacts with the generation-equality check in a subtle way (a stable-but-changed generation is terminal, not retried), creating a complex state machine that could mask bugs.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either define `PlanRelationSnapshot`'s required fields inline in this plan (or cite the exact section of acaf where they are specified) and add an explicit dependency edge on acaf so the sign-transaction work is unblocked.
evidence: "'relation_snapshot' is child acaf's exact `PlanRelationSnapshot`"
No dependency edge on acaf is listed anywhere in the plan
E6 taxonomy #9: references a sibling/child ticket's files with no dependency edge
Executor cannot write the dataclass or its equality check without knowing PlanRelationSnapshot's fields
findings.finding: PlanReviewGeneration references `child acaf's exact PlanRelationSnapshot` without defining or importing the type in this plan, leaving the executor unable to implement or test the struct until acaf exists; no dependency edge is declared.
findings.location: Implementation — 'Own one pre-sign generation transaction' paragraph; AC 'A stable generation equal to the initial generation signs and commits under one lock'; Verification — `tests/unit/test_plan_review_sign_transaction.py`
scenarios: Executor starts implementing PlanReviewGeneration and has no definition of PlanRelationSnapshot to use
test_plan_review_sign_transaction.py cannot construct valid fixture objects for the struct
If acaf lands later, the generation equality check may need revision, creating a silent regression window
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Depends on: acaf' line to the plan header and add a one-line stub definition such as `PlanRelationSnapshot = <acaf-defined dataclass; fields: X, Y, Z>` in the generation-transaction paragraph so an executor has enough to write the struct and tests without blocking on acaf's completion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a named pytest path for the rollback test (e.g. `tests/unit/test_plan_review_phase_reducer.py::test_rollback_raw_reduct_drops_phase` or a dedicated `tests/unit/test_plan_review_rollback.py`) to the Verification section so the rollback AC has a concrete proving command.
evidence: All other ACs map to a named file (e.g. `tests/unit/test_plan_review_phase_reducer.py`, `tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_plan_review_sign_transaction.py`)
The rollback test is only referenced as 'the named integration and rollback tests' in a catch-all `make check` bullet with no file path
AC text says 'The rollback test proves ...' implying a specific test, but no file is specified
findings.finding: The rollback acceptance criterion ('The rollback test proves old-code raw-event re-reduction drops the unknown field and forward upgrade reboots it from compiled status, with the documented precision-loss window') has no named test file in the Verification section, unlike every other AC which maps to an explicit pytest path.
findings.location: Verification section — last bullet 'Run `make check` and the full non-LLM test suite, including the named integration and rollback tests'
scenarios: An agent implementing the plan does not know where to place the rollback test
A CI failure in the rollback scenario produces no targeted pytest command to reproduce it
A reviewer cannot verify the rollback AC without knowing which file to inspect

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether 'every plan-review validity result' excludes completion-verifier calls (i.e. restrict the AC to 'every plan-review-gate validity result') or explicitly state that completion-verifier uses a distinct compute_validity branch that produces a phase_status of a sentinel/absent value without invoking phase parsers.
evidence: 'return phase_status on every later result'
'Completion-verifier calls never invoke phase, floor, or pin parsers'
'DerivedPlanReviewHealth.phase_status is present on every plan-review validity result'
findings.finding: The Implementation says `DerivedPlanReviewHealth.phase_status` is returned 'on every later result' from compute_validity's plan-review branch, but the Implementation also says 'Completion-verifier calls never invoke phase, floor, or pin parsers.' The AC 'DerivedPlanReviewHealth.phase_status is present on every plan-review validity result' may contradict the completion-verifier isolation if the completion verifier shares compute_validity's plan-review code path — it cannot both always produce phase_status (requiring phase parsing) and never invoke phase parsers.
findings.location: Implementation bullet 5 ('Extend 75de's compute_validity…') and AC 'DerivedPlanReviewHealth.phase_status is present on every plan-review validity result' vs. AC 'Completion-verifier validity never calls phase, floor, or pin parsers'
scenarios: Implementer wires completion-verifier through the same compute_validity plan-review branch (satisfying the 'every result' AC) but then violates the isolation AC; or creates a separate path that never produces phase_status, violating the 'every result' AC.
findings.suggested_fix: Amend the AC to read: 'DerivedPlanReviewHealth.phase_status is present on every plan-review-gate validity result; completion-verifier calls use a separate branch and do not populate phase_status.' Then add a complementary AC: 'The completion-verifier branch of compute_validity does not call validate_review_phase_metadata, manifest_review_phase(), or manifest_priority_floor().'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one counter metric (or equivalent structured telemetry hook) for each new failure domain: bootstrap events, generation-retry/abort events, and phase_status=malformed/incompatible events; reference these in the `docs/plan-review-gate.md` observability section.
evidence: Only structured log events named: plan_review_phase_bootstrap (INFO), plan_review_generation_retry (WARN), plan_review_sign_aborted (ERROR).
ABSENCE: no metric increments, no alert thresholds, no dashboard/tracing hooks for any of the three new failure domains.
'docs/plan-review-gate.md' sections listed do not include an observability / runbook section.
findings.finding: The three new failure domains — phase bootstrap, generation transaction (retry/stale/abort), and manifest phase/floor parse errors — are covered only by structured log events. No metrics (counters, error-rate gauges) or alert hooks are specified, leaving operators with no way to detect elevated bootstrap failures, signing-abort spikes, or systematic floor-incompatibility issues without manually tailing logs.
findings.location: Implementation section across: bootstrap ('Emit one structured INFO plan_review_phase_bootstrap'), signing transaction ('emit structured WARN plan_review_generation_retry / structured ERROR plan_review_sign_aborted'), and phase compatibility ('phase_status=malformed/incompatible').
scenarios: plan_review_sign_aborted fires repeatedly due to a tracker misconfiguration; without a counter metric no alert fires and operators discover the issue only on manual log review after attestations go missing.
Systematic execution-floor incompatibility (malformed manifests) silently accumulates as phase_status=malformed results; no metric makes this trend visible.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider (advisory) documenting a thin MVP path that validates the `PlanReviewGeneration` locking/retry protocol in isolation (e.g. with a generation struct that omits phase) before layering on the phase/floor enforcement, so that concurrency correctness can be verified independently of the phase policy.
evidence: All ten implementation bullets ship atomically with no documented thin-slice path.
The generation transaction's correctness risk (concurrency, lock semantics) is qualitatively different from the phase/floor policy risk (business rules, manifest grammar).
Sequencing checklist: 'judge whether a thin vertical-slice / evidence-gated MVP de-risks the riskiest piece first, versus a horizontal big-bang.'
The plan's Verification section names all test files at once with no phased gate.
findings.finding: No thin vertical-slice / evidence-gated MVP is described to de-risk the most novel and highest-risk piece — the `PlanReviewGeneration` locking/retry protocol — before the full feature ships. The generation transaction introduces new concurrency semantics (under-lock HEAD re-verification, three-retry outer loop, `LockTimeout` handling) that are independent of the phase/floor policy yet ship atomically with it. If the locking design turns out to be wrong, the entire feature must be rolled back together.
findings.location: Implementation — 'Own one pre-sign generation transaction' and 'Before signing, retain one immutable initial_generation' bullets
scenarios: The locking retry logic has a subtle bug (e.g. retry counter miscounted under certain HEAD-equality conditions); diagnosing it requires untangling it from the simultaneous phase enforcement changes.
A reviewer or implementer cannot validate generation-transaction correctness independently from phase-manifest correctness during code review.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm `PlanReviewGeneration` has ≥3 independent call-sites at this ticket's ship boundary, or replace it with an inline named-tuple / local assertion in the sign-transaction path.
evidence: Plan: 'PlanReviewGeneration is {phase, priority_floor, own_material, relation_snapshot, ticket_store_revision} where ... equality is dataclass equality across every field.'
Codebase grep for 'PlanReviewGeneration' returns zero results — no existing call-sites.

The only described call-sites are 'Full review collects the initial generation before the LLM; drift refresh and re-sign collect it immediately before their non-LLM probe' — all within the same sign-transaction boundary, not independent consumers.
Rule-of-Three requires ≥3 independent call-sites before a named abstraction is justified.
findings.finding: `PlanReviewGeneration` is a new named dataclass with only one described production call-site (the sign-transaction path), making it a premature abstraction that does not satisfy the Rule-of-Three.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Own one pre-sign generation transaction' paragraph; AC 'A stable generation equal to the initial generation signs and commits under one lock'
scenarios: An implementer must define, test, and maintain a five-field dataclass equality type that is consumed only within a single signing context; the same goal could be achieved with a local tuple or inline assertions.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm `SidecarReviewPhaseError` has ≥3 independent catch-sites across the codebase at ship, or inline the error translation at the one call-site instead of introducing a new exception class.
evidence: Codebase grep for 'SidecarReviewPhaseError' returns zero results — the type does not yet exist.
Only one call-site is described in the plan: the `parse_review_phase_metadata` function in sidecar.py.

The codebase already shows a pattern of catching `ManifestFormatError` at call-sites rather than translating it to new exception types — e.g., `manifest_deps`, `manifest_disabled_builtins` parse without a translator exception.
Rule-of-Three: one call-site does not justify a named exception class.
findings.finding: `SidecarReviewPhaseError` is a single-use exception class introduced solely to translate `ManifestFormatError` into a sidecar-domain error at one call-site (`sidecar.parse_review_phase_metadata`), which does not satisfy the Rule-of-Three and is a premature abstraction.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Add sidecar.parse_review_phase_metadata(payload) -> ReviewPhaseMetadata; it passes raw JSON values to manifest.validate_review_phase_metadata and translates ManifestFormatError to SidecarReviewPhaseError'
scenarios: Callers of `sidecar.parse_review_phase_metadata` must import and handle a new exception type that is mechanically equivalent to catching `ManifestFormatError` with a context note.
findings.suggested_fix: Raise `ManifestFormatError` directly (or re-raise with added context) from `sidecar.parse_review_phase_metadata` rather than defining `SidecarReviewPhaseError`; callers already know how to handle `ManifestFormatError`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific file (e.g., `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/orchestrator.py` or a new `generation.py`) where `PlanReviewGeneration` will be defined.
evidence: Plan text: 'PlanReviewGeneration is {phase, priority_floor, own_material, relation_snapshot, ticket_store_revision} where relation_snapshot is child acaf's exact PlanRelationSnapshot and equality is dataclass equality across every field.'
No file path is given for PlanReviewGeneration in the plan body.
Confirmed absent via grep across src/rebar/llm/plan_review/: no file currently defines PlanReviewGeneration.

The plan names specific modules for every other new type: validate_review_phase_metadata in manifest.py, parse_review_phase_metadata in sidecar.py, PlanRelationSnapshot in relation_snapshot.py (acaf), DerivedPlanReviewHealth in attest.py (75de) — but PlanReviewGeneration has no home.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `PlanReviewGeneration` as a new dataclass (with fields `phase`, `priority_floor`, `own_material`, `relation_snapshot`, `ticket_store_revision`) but does not name which file it will live in. All other new symbols are traceable to specific files, but this one is not — leaving implementers without a clear home for it and no file-impact target to hash in the manifest.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Own one pre-sign generation transaction'
scenarios: An implementer must guess whether PlanReviewGeneration belongs in orchestrator.py, attest.py, a new generation.py module, or relation_snapshot.py.
Manifest dep-pinning of this file becomes ambiguous — the wrong file gets hashed.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the implementation section: 'Define `PlanReviewGeneration` in `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/orchestrator.py` alongside the existing pre-sign generation logic, or in a new `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sign_transaction.py` leaf that owns the full retry/lock contract.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly confirm that `sidecar.py` is a named edit target for this ticket's `plan_review_result_v2` phase/floor key additions and `parse_review_phase_metadata` (distinct from 75de's `reviewed_related_material` edit to the same file).
evidence: Plan text: 'new full reviews always write JSON review_phase as lowercase string planning or execution; planning omits priority_floor; execution writes JSON number priority_floor: 0.8'
sidecar.py confirmed existing with build_payload at line 394.
75de file_impact lists sidecar.py for reviewed_related_material extension only; 5967 plan text additionally requires phase/floor keys in the same payload.
Plan says 'Add sidecar.parse_review_phase_metadata(payload) -> ReviewPhaseMetadata' — also in sidecar.py, not listed.
findings.finding: The plan says `sidecar.emit` and `build_payload` gain `reviewed_related_material` as a new keyword-only parameter (per 75de). This plan (5967) also extends these functions to emit `review_phase`/`priority_floor` into the sidecar payload. However, `sidecar.py` is already listed in 75de's file_impact for the `reviewed_related_material` extension. The plan for 5967 does not list `sidecar.py` in any explicit file_impact, yet requires additive changes to `emit` and `build_payload` beyond what 75de alone delivers. This could cause a missed edit if implementers treat 75de's sidecar work as complete for that file.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Persist review-time phase data for failed-sign recovery'
scenarios: An implementer completing 75de's sidecar work may ship sidecar.py without the phase/floor keys because 5967 didn't explicitly include it in its own stated file scope.
The sidecar v2 payload ends up missing review_phase keys, breaking the resign recovery path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify precisely what `ticket_store_revision` maps to in the existing codebase (e.g. `signing.head_sha()` of the tracker branch, the ticket's dir-hash from `reducer._cache.compute_dir_hash`, or a new primitive), and confirm that the chosen primitive is both cheap to read and correctly scoped to the signing ticket.
evidence: Plan: 'PlanReviewGeneration is {phase, priority_floor, own_material, relation_snapshot, ticket_store_revision}'
ABSENCE: `ticket_store_revision` appears nowhere in the codebase (`src/rebar/`, `src/rebar/_store/`, `src/rebar/reducer/`).
Existing primitives: `signing.head_sha()` returns the git HEAD SHA (whole-repo, not per-ticket); per-ticket event count is available from the reducer but not named as a revision concept.

Plan: 'read clean tracker HEAD, collect a fresh generation, read HEAD again; unequal HEADs emit structured WARN' — this conflates tracker HEAD (a git ref) with a ticket-specific revision, without specifying which is `ticket_store_revision`.
findings.finding: `PlanReviewGeneration` includes a `ticket_store_revision` field, but no such concept exists in the codebase. The plan does not specify which existing primitive backs this field (e.g. `signing.head_sha()`, a per-ticket event count, or a new git-ref read), leaving this a capability assertion that cannot be verified from the plan text alone.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Own one pre-sign generation transaction for ordinary review, drift refresh, and re-sign. `PlanReviewGeneration` is `{phase, priority_floor, own_material, relation_snapshot, ticket_store_revision}`'
scenarios: If `ticket_store_revision` maps to the tracker branch HEAD SHA, implementors need to confirm that whole-repo HEAD equality is a sufficient proxy for 'the ticket's events didn't change' (it is not, if other tickets were written between reads).
If `ticket_store_revision` is a per-ticket event count or dir-hash, implementors need to confirm that primitive is cheap enough to read twice per signing attempt.
findings.suggested_fix: The tracker branch HEAD SHA (`signing.head_sha(tracker_dir)`) is the existing closest primitive — clarify if that is the intended mapping and note that it's whole-repo (other-ticket writes will also trigger a mismatch).

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'gates/plan-review.yaml maps it [review_phase] into the decide step' as if there is a single plan_review_decide invocation in the YAML, but reading the actual plan-review.yaml shows TWO separate plan_review_decide invocations: one inside the code_grounded branch (step id 'decide', lines ~167-175) and one in the non-agentic else branch (step id 'decide', lines ~247-255). Updating only one of the two YAML wiring sites would leave the non-agentic branch passing plan_review_decide without review_phase, silently dropping execution-floor enforcement for all non-code-grounded reviews.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Thread phase through the production workflow: ... llm/workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml maps it into the decide step'
findings.suggested_fix: In plan-review.yaml, add `review_phase: ${{ steps.precheck.outputs.review_phase }}` to the `with:` block of every `plan_review_decide` step — currently at least two call sites exist (the code_grounded branch decide and the else-branch decide).

findings.finding: The `review_phase` threading from `plan_review_precheck` outputs into `plan_review_decide` is specified in prose but has no co-located mechanism in `gates/plan-review.yaml`. Both decide call-sites in the verified YAML have no `review_phase:` key in their `with:` blocks, and the workflow-level `inputs:` section (verified: only `ticket_id` and `probe_criteria`) has no `review_phase` input. The plan says 'gates/plan-review.yaml maps it into the decide step' but does not state which exact YAML lines to add, nor does it add `review_phase` to the `plan_review_decide_input.schema.json` (which currently has `additionalProperties: true` but omits the field from `properties`).
findings.location: Implementation § 'Thread phase through the production workflow'; src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml lines 167-176 and 247-256 (both decide with-blocks); src/rebar/schemas/plan_review_decide_input.schema.json
findings.suggested_fix: Add `review_phase: ${{ steps.precheck.outputs.review_phase }}` to both `plan_review_decide` `with:` blocks in plan-review.yaml (lines ~172-176 and ~252-256). Add `review_phase` to `plan_review_decide_input.schema.json` `properties` as `{ "type": "string", "enum": ["planning", "execution"] }` and add it to `required`.

findings.finding: The modification protocol for the local `_threshold_for` closure inside `orchestrator.pass3_over_findings` is stated in the Pass-3 section but not co-located with the closure's definition. The plan says 'the local `_threshold_for` closure captures that parameter, calls `_criteria.threshold_for(...)`, and destructures `(block_threshold, blocking_enabled)`. When `execution_review and blocking_enabled`, it returns `(max(block_threshold, 0.80), blocking_enabled)`.' However, `orchestrator.pass3_over_findings` (verified at lines 678-703) is a module-level function with a fixed `(findings, verifs)` signature, and its `_threshold_for` closure (line 694-695) currently delegates to `_criteria.threshold_for` with no extra parameters. The plan does not specify whether `execution_review` is a new keyword parameter on `orchestrator.pass3_over_findings` itself or is captured from the outer `plan_review_decide` scope.
findings.location: Implementation § 'Thread phase through the production workflow' (Pass-3 paragraph); src/rebar/llm/plan_review/orchestrator.py lines 678-703

findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel `PlanReviewGeneration` pre-sign transaction with an outside-the-lock HEAD-equality retry loop (read HEAD → collect generation → read HEAD → compare; retry up to 3× on instability) without establishing why the existing write-lock + `event_append` retry infrastructure is insufficient or how this outer loop composes safely with the inner retries already present in `_with_index_lock_retry` / `_with_transient_head_retry` and the 2-attempt `lock.acquire` in compact.py.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Own one pre-sign generation transaction' and 'Before signing, retain one immutable initial_generation... Retry outside the lock at most three times only for an unstable tracker revision'

findings.finding: The plan says 'workflow_ops passes the compiled review phase into this parameter' (the new execution_review keyword arg on orchestrator.pass3_over_findings), but plan_review_decide in workflow_ops.py currently only receives findings/verifications/det_blocking/det_advisory from its StepContext inputs — it has no access to the compiled plan_review_phase field. The plan does not specify whether (a) plan_review_phase is threaded as a new workflow step input from plan_review_precheck, (b) plan_review_decide reads compiled state via _reads.show_ticket, or (c) some other mechanism. Without specifying this, workflow_ops.py's required edit is underspecified.
findings.location: Implementation section, Pass-3 bullet: 'workflow_ops passes the compiled review phase into this parameter'; workflow_ops.py::plan_review_decide (line 484)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rewrite the advisory/novelty/completion AC to enumerate the specific observable invariants (e.g. 'advisory-only criteria have `blocking_enabled=False` and the threshold is NOT floored to 0.80; novelty and completion criteria thresholds are unchanged; deterministic structural failures remain blocking regardless of execution_review flag').
evidence: 'retain their documented behavior' is not a specific observable assertion; it points to an unspecified document rather than stating what passes/fails
Other ACs in the same list state concrete outcomes: 'floors only the float threshold when blocking is enabled', 'returns the original threshold pair unchanged'
No document reference (file path, section name) is given for the 'documented behavior'
findings.finding: The AC 'Advisory-only criteria, novelty/completion floors, deterministic failures, and visible sub-floor findings retain their documented behavior' uses 'retain their documented behavior' as the success condition — an indirect reference to external documentation rather than a specific observable outcome, making it unevaluable in-session without hunting down the referenced docs.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Advisory-only criteria, novelty/completion floors, deterministic failures, and visible sub-floor findings retain their documented behavior.'
scenarios: Test author writing test_plan_review_execution_floor.py must guess what assertions to write for advisory/novelty/completion behaviors
A passing test suite that only checks the new path leaves these behaviors implicitly trusted rather than explicitly asserted

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rewrite this AC to state the specific observable outcome(s) the integration test must assert (e.g. 'returns a signed result with the correct phase field, the appended ticket event contains the signature, and the commit succeeds under one write lock with a matching HEAD').
evidence: 'covers … end to end' describes what is exercised, not what is proven or asserted
Contrast with neighboring ACs that state outcomes: 'HEAD instability retries at most three times with exact WARN fields, then returns retryable unsigned'
No assertion outcome (e.g. 'returns signed result', 'appended event has correct phase field') is stated
findings.finding: The AC 'The production-path integration test covers stable collection, under-lock HEAD re-verification, signature append, and commit end to end' is effort-framed ('covers … end to end') rather than stating a specific observable outcome, making it indistinguishable from a coverage statement.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'The production-path integration test covers stable collection, under-lock HEAD re-verification, signature append, and commit end to end.'
scenarios: A test that calls the production path and asserts only that no exception is raised would satisfy this AC as written
A reviewer cannot tell from the AC whether the test asserts the signature event's content, the commit hash, or only the absence of an error

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete rollback mechanism for disabling claim/close phase enforcement (e.g., the exact config key, flag name, or deploy step), and add an AC that tests toggling enforcement off via that mechanism without requiring a raw-event re-reduction.
evidence: 'Rollback disables claim/close enforcement first' — no mechanism described.
The AC tests old-code raw-event re-reduction but does not test the enforcement-disable step itself.
If the only rollback is a code redeploy, this is not 'cheap' per the T12 criterion.
findings.finding: The rollback step 'Rollback disables claim/close enforcement first' is stated as a narrative requirement but no concrete mechanism (config key, flag, code path, CLI command) is specified, making the rollback path untestable and potentially slow.
findings.location: Implementation — Rollback paragraph; AC: 'The rollback test proves old-code raw-event re-reduction drops the unknown field...'
scenarios: An incident requires fast rollback; the operator doesn't know which config key or deploy step disables enforcement, causing extended blocking of plan reviews during the rollback window.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the user-facing error messages and recovery instructions for each new terminal rejection state (incompatible re-sign, malformed phase/floor, `plan_review_generation_changed`), and add AC lines confirming these messages are surfaced to the developer.
evidence: 'A stable fresh generation unequal to the initial generation is terminal unsigned stale (plan_review_generation_changed) and requires re-review; it is not retried.' — no user-facing message or recovery steps described.
'resign_plan_review ... refuses incompatible/malformed re-sign' — no description of what the developer sees or how they recover.
The AC for re-sign tests correctness of refusal but not the user-visible output.
findings.finding: The plan introduces new terminal rejection states for developers (incompatible re-sign, malformed phase/floor, terminal-stale `plan_review_generation_changed`) but does not specify the user-facing error messages or recovery steps for those states.
findings.location: Implementation — Re-sign paragraph; PlanReviewGeneration transaction paragraph; AC: 'Re-sign parses the exact v2 JSON phase fields...' and 'Any stable phase, own-material, relationship, related-fingerprint, or generation-field change returns terminal unsigned stale.'
scenarios: A developer's re-sign is refused as incompatible; they have no actionable message about which field changed or how to fix it.
A generation-changed terminal state occurs mid-workflow; the developer sees an opaque unsigned INDETERMINATE with no guidance to initiate re-review.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC verifying that `sidecar.parse_review_phase_metadata` translates every `ManifestFormatError` from `validate_review_phase_metadata` into a `SidecarReviewPhaseError` (e.g. 'sidecar.parse_review_phase_metadata raises SidecarReviewPhaseError, not ManifestFormatError, for every malformed payload variant').
evidence: 'Add sidecar.parse_review_phase_metadata(payload) -> ReviewPhaseMetadata; it passes raw JSON values to manifest.validate_review_phase_metadata and translates ManifestFormatError to SidecarReviewPhaseError'
No AC mentions SidecarReviewPhaseError or the error-translation behavior
findings.finding: The Implementation describes `sidecar.parse_review_phase_metadata(payload) -> ReviewPhaseMetadata` with a specific error-translation behavior (ManifestFormatError → SidecarReviewPhaseError), but no Acceptance Criterion verifies this error-translation contract. The re-sign AC only verifies that re-sign 'parses via sidecar.parse_review_phase_metadata and refuses incompatible/malformed variants,' which tests the happy/incompatible paths but not the error-type translation itself.
findings.location: Implementation bullet 6 ('Persist review-time phase data for failed-sign recovery…') and Acceptance Criteria section ('Re-sign parses the exact v2 JSON phase fields through sidecar.parse_review_phase_metadata…')
scenarios: Implementation ships sidecar.parse_review_phase_metadata that raises ManifestFormatError directly (skipping translation), satisfying the re-sign AC but violating the described contract — callers expecting SidecarReviewPhaseError will fail silently.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC verifying that `manifest_priority_floor()` returns `None` for a planning manifest (where the priority-floor line is absent) and that this is distinguishable from a zero floor.
evidence: 'manifest_priority_floor() returns None only when absent'
AC 'Legacy phase-less attestations parse as planning' covers absence of the phase line but not the floor accessor's None return
No AC references manifest_priority_floor() or its None-when-absent behavior
findings.finding: The Implementation specifies that `manifest_priority_floor()` 'returns None only when absent,' but no Acceptance Criterion verifies this null-return-on-absence behavior. The manifest ACs cover format correctness, syntax errors, and legacy-absent planning parsing, but none verifies the specific accessor's behavior when the floor line is absent (e.g. in a planning manifest).
findings.location: Implementation bullet 3 ('manifest_priority_floor() returns None only when absent') and Acceptance Criteria section (manifest-related ACs)
scenarios: manifest_priority_floor() returns 0.0 or raises on absence rather than None; downstream code that relies on None-check to distinguish planning from execution breaks silently.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a sixth required section to `docs/plan-review-gate.md` (and the corresponding `test_plan_review_docs.py` assertion) covering the `plan_review_result_v2` sidecar schema: valid/malformed key combinations, legacy-absent semantics, and the mapping to `ReviewPhaseMetadata`.
evidence: 'Persist review-time phase data for failed-sign recovery with an exact additive `plan_review_result_v2` schema: new full reviews always write JSON `review_phase` as lowercase string...'
The five required documentation sections listed in both the Implementation and the docs AC do not include the sidecar v2 format.
test_plan_review_docs.py verifies only the five enumerated sections, so the sidecar contract gap will not be caught by that test.
findings.finding: The `plan_review_result_v2` sidecar schema is a new external data contract critical to recovery (re-sign after failed sign), but it is not listed among the five required sections in `docs/plan-review-gate.md`. The AC for documentation covers only: phase/floor manifest contract, phase_status compatibility table, fixed-0.80 rationale, PlanReviewGeneration transaction, and rollback precision-loss — leaving the sidecar v2 format unspecified in the planned docs.
findings.location: Implementation — 'Persist review-time phase data for failed-sign recovery with an exact additive plan_review_result_v2 schema'; AC: 'docs/plan-review-gate.md contains sections for...'
scenarios: An operator performing manual sidecar recovery or writing a migration script for legacy sidecars has no canonical reference for which key combinations are valid vs. malformed in v2, or what `plan_review_result_v2` looks like.
A downstream tool parsing sidecars for audit/reporting purposes cannot determine the v2 contract from published documentation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a per-read deadline (or an overall signing-path wall-clock budget) for tracker HEAD reads in the retry loop, and ensure a timeout on those reads is classified as a retryable unsigned outcome with the same observability as `LockTimeout`.
evidence: 'Retry outside the lock at most three times' — retry count bounded, but per-attempt I/O timeout absent.
'LockTimeout returns retryable unsigned' — lock acquisition is time-bounded, but the tracker HEAD reads themselves have no stated timeout.
ABSENCE: no per-read deadline, no overall signing-path timeout, no circuit-breaker for a slow tracker
findings.finding: The generation-transaction retry loop introduces up to three rounds of tracker HEAD reads (I/O), plus the under-lock HEAD re-read and signature append, but no timeout bound is specified for any individual tracker read or for the total signing operation. An indefinitely slow or hung tracker can block the signing path without a time-bounded escape.
findings.location: Implementation section, signing transaction: 'Retry outside the lock at most three times only for an unstable tracker revision... Under the global write lock, require the same clean HEAD and exact generation, then append and commit the signature atomically. LockTimeout returns retryable unsigned without writing.'
scenarios: Tracker is reachable but very slow (e.g. NFS stall, remote store latency spike): each of the three retry attempts blocks indefinitely at the HEAD read, hanging the review workflow with no signal to the caller until OS-level timeout.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
